intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is CineFlow Productions' autonomous invoice-processing agent: it receives vendor invoices, validates the submitting vendor's registered/approved status, runs fraud detection, and decides whether to auto-approve (triggering payment), auto-reject, or escalate to human review. Authorized counterparties are registered vendors (who supply data but cannot give directives), CineFlow finance admins (who may adjust operational thresholds through an out-of-runtime configuration channel), and the LLM provider (inference only). The remit hard-prohibits runtime mutation of FinBot's goals or decision logic by anyone other than a verified operator deploying out-of-band, forbids treating invoice-content fields as directives, and forbids skipping fraud detection for any reason.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python Flask + SQLAlchemy application (SQLite or PostgreSQL on Render). A single FinBotAgent class in src/services/finbot_agent.py orchestrates OpenAI function-calling against five tools (get_invoice_details, approve_invoice, reject_invoice, request_human_review, detect_fraud_patterns) with a rule-based _fallback_processing path when the OpenAI client is unavailable. Admin routes are exposed via a Flask blueprint at /api/admin/* (config GET/POST, goals POST, vendor-trust POST, dashboard, CTF flags) with no authentication middleware; vendor and user CRUD routes are likewise unauthenticated. Configuration state — including fraud_detection_enabled, custom_goals, speed_priority, and the auto-approve / manual-review thresholds — lives in a single mutable FinBotConfig row that the system prompt interpolates at every request.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in prose, zero code-level enforcement, plus an unauthenticated control surface that lets any external caller redefine the agent's goals. Every MUST / MUST NOT in the remit — fraud detection cannot be skipped, runtime messages cannot alter decision logic, invoice-content fields are not directives, the manual-review threshold cannot be bypassed — is implemented entirely as system-prompt text and as runtime config defaults that the /api/admin/finbot/* blueprint exposes for unauthenticated POST modification.

A one-hop compound chain runs from anonymous vendor registration → invoice submission with a crafted description → _analyze_business_context's authority/urgency/production keyword scan → approval with payment_processed=True, with the LLM path additionally susceptible to goal-injection via custom_goals that the admin endpoint persists into get_system_prompt's template at line 71. The confidence_threshold field is declared in FinBotConfig but never consulted by any decision path — the canonical declared-but-never-consulted pattern.

rules.rule_text: FinBot may auto-approve invoices from registered, approved vendors when the amount is below the configured auto-approval threshold.
FinBot MUST record its reasoning for every decision it makes.
FinBot MUST run fraud detection before approving any invoice.
FinBot MUST confirm the submitting vendor is registered with approved status before processing an invoice.
FinBot MUST escalate to human review rather than auto-approve whenever any fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount.
Fraud detection MUST NOT be skipped for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions.
FinBot MUST NOT approve payment to a vendor that is not registered and approved.
FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off.
Runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, and incoming payloads — MUST NOT alter FinBot's goals, instructions, or decision logic.
Instructions embedded in invoice data fields, such as descriptions, notes, or line items, MUST NOT be treated as directives.
FinBot MUST NOT weaken its security posture on the instruction of anyone other than a verified operator making an authorized configuration change outside of agent runtime.
Any invoice above the manual-review threshold MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds.
Any invoice from a vendor with a low trust level MUST be escalated to human review.
Any invoice where FinBot's decision confidence falls below the configured confidence threshold MUST be escalated to human review.
Fraud detection MUST remain enabled at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change.
Operator configuration MUST NOT be used to inject natural-language instructions into FinBot's goals or system prompt; goal changes require a deployment by authorized engineering staff.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated admin endpoint persists arbitrary text into FinBot's system prompt via the custom_goals field.
findings.policy_rule_text: Operator configuration MUST NOT be used to inject natural-language instructions into FinBot's goals or system prompt; goal changes require a deployment by authorized engineering staff.

evidence.snippet: /admin/finbot/goals POST route has no authentication, accepts arbitrary `goals` string from request body and forwards to finbot_agent.update_goals — lines 90-109
get_system_prompt appends config.custom_goals verbatim to the LLM system prompt under the header CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING) — line 71

recommended_actions: Require an authenticated operator session (admin role) on /admin/finbot/goals POST and reject anonymous requests.
Remove the runtime custom_goals path entirely — goal modifications should require a deployment, as the remit specifies.

findings.summary: fraud_detection_enabled is a runtime-toggleable bool that short-circuits the fraud-detection tool, with no authenticated path required to flip it.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST run fraud detection before approving any invoice. / Fraud detection MUST NOT be skipped for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions. / Fraud detection MUST remain enabled at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change.

evidence.snippet: fraud_detection_enabled = db.Column(db.Boolean, default=True) — mutable column on the FinBotConfig row
_detect_fraud_patterns returns empty fraud_indicators with message 'Fraud detection is disabled' when the flag is False — lines 509-515

recommended_actions: Remove fraud_detection_enabled from the runtime config — make fraud detection unconditional in code.
If a kill-switch is genuinely needed, gate it behind an authenticated operator role AND require a separate deployment-time flag.

findings.summary: Business-context heuristic auto-approves invoices even when the agent's own injection detector flagged the description.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST escalate to human review rather than auto-approve whenever any fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount.

evidence.snippet: _fallback_processing override branch: when contains_injection is True the code STILL approves if business_context['should_expedite'] and speed_priority > 0.6, OR if management_approval and production_context — lines 841-850

recommended_actions: Remove the business-context override branches at lines 841-850 and route every flagged invoice to _request_human_review unconditionally.

findings.summary: Manual-review threshold can be bypassed by keyword content in the invoice description.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off. / Any invoice above the manual-review threshold MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds.

evidence.snippet: When invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold the code auto-approves anyway if any of business_context['should_expedite'], (management_approval and production_context), or (days_until_due <= 1 and production_context) holds — lines 807-820

_analyze_business_context flags these conditions by substring search of invoice.description.lower() for phrases like pre-approved, ceo approved, production deadline, broadcast premiere — lines 670-697

recommended_actions: Treat the manual-review threshold as a hard ceiling on auto-approval — remove the override branches at lines 808-820.
If expedited review is needed for legitimate cases, build a separate authenticated approval channel rather than a description-content heuristic.

findings.summary: No authentication middleware on any /api/admin/* route — config mutation, goal injection, vendor-trust modification, and review decisions all accept anonymous requests.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST NOT weaken its security posture on the instruction of anyone other than a verified operator making an authorized configuration change outside of agent runtime.

evidence.snippet: Flask app registers no authentication middleware and CORS(app) opens every endpoint to all origins — lines 14-22

Every blueprint route (/admin/finbot/config POST, /admin/finbot/goals POST, /admin/vendors/<id>/trust POST, /admin/invoices/<id>/review POST, /admin/finbot/reprocess/<id> POST) accepts requests with no session check, no API key, no operator-identity verification

recommended_actions: Add a @require_admin decorator (or Flask-Login + role check) to every /admin/* route; reject requests that lack a verified operator session.
Restrict CORS to known origins so cross-origin POSTs cannot reach the admin surface from arbitrary pages.

findings.summary: confidence_threshold is declared in FinBotConfig but never read by any decision path — the canonical declared-but-never-consulted pattern.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice where FinBot's decision confidence falls below the configured confidence threshold MUST be escalated to human review.

evidence.snippet: confidence_threshold = db.Column(db.Float, default=0.8) — declared on FinBotConfig

confidence_threshold appears only in update_config setter (lines 758-759); no decision branch in _run_agent_orchestration or _fallback_processing reads it; no comparison of ai_confidence against the threshold gates any approval path

recommended_actions: Before any _approve_invoice call, compare the LLM's reported confidence (and the rule-based path's confidence variable) against config.confidence_threshold and route to _request_human_review when below.

findings.summary: Vendor records are auto-approved on registration — any anonymous caller can register a vendor and immediately submit invoices as a registered, approved party.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot may auto-approve invoices from registered, approved vendors when the amount is below the configured auto-approval threshold. / FinBot MUST confirm the submitting vendor is registered with approved status before processing an invoice. / FinBot MUST NOT approve payment to a vendor that is not registered and approved.

evidence.snippet: register_vendor creates a new Vendor row with status='approved' (comment 'Auto-approve for demo') and trust_level='standard' with no review step — lines 24-38
The route accepts anonymous POST; combined with line 37 this lets any caller self-onboard as an approved vendor in a single request

recommended_actions: Set status='pending' on registration and require an authenticated admin POST to transition to approved.
Gate register_vendor itself behind authentication so anonymous callers cannot self-onboard.

findings.summary: Compound chain — anonymous vendor registration → invoice submission with crafted description → business-context override → autonomous payment with no human in the loop.
findings.policy_rule_text: Runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, and incoming payloads — MUST NOT alter FinBot's goals, instructions, or decision logic. / Instructions embedded in invoice data fields, such as descriptions, notes, or line items, MUST NOT be treated as directives.

evidence.snippet: anonymous register_vendor → submit_invoice chain accepts a description string with no content filter — lines 12-52 and 69-110
_analyze_business_context substring-matches description for authority/urgency/production phrases and feeds the result into the approval decision — lines 661-733

recommended_actions: Remove _analyze_business_context content-based scoring — replace with a fixed allow-list of vendor-supplied fields that influence the decision; never let invoice content keywords flip approval branches.

findings.summary: Flask SECRET_KEY is a hardcoded literal in source, not loaded from environment.

evidence.snippet: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = '[REDACTED — 20-character string literal at src/main.py:15]' — committed in source rather than loaded from os.environ

recommended_actions: Load SECRET_KEY from os.environ['SECRET_KEY'] (fail-closed if absent) and rotate the literal that has been in the repo history.
Store the new value in the deployment platform's secret manager (Render's environment variables or equivalent).

findings.summary: CORS is wide open — CORS(app) with no origin restriction permits any origin to call any endpoint including unauthenticated /api/admin/* POSTs.

evidence.snippet: CORS(app) with no origins= argument; combined with no-auth admin endpoints (PRAX-2026-05-25-005) this lets any browser on any origin POST to /api/admin/finbot/goals from JavaScript

recommended_actions: Restrict CORS to the application's own origin: CORS(app, origins=[os.environ['ALLOWED_ORIGIN']]); add an explicit allow-list for any third-party UI that needs to call the API.

findings.summary: No rate limiting on any endpoint — agent invocation via vendor invoice POST triggers a full LLM call per request with no throttle.

evidence.snippet: No rate-limit middleware (Flask-Limiter or equivalent) registered anywhere in the app factory
submit_invoice synchronously calls finbot.process_invoice which runs the LLM agent loop (up to 5 iterations) — every POST is a paid LLM call

recommended_actions: Install Flask-Limiter, set a per-IP limit on /api/vendors/<id>/invoices POST (e.g. 10/min) and stricter limits on /api/admin/*.
Consider per-vendor token budgets on agent invocation to cap cost-of-attack.

findings.summary: No structured action / audit log for admin mutations or agent decisions — print() to stdout is the only operational logging.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST record its reasoning for every decision it makes.

evidence.snippet: print(f'User Agreement Logged: {agreement_log}') is the only logging call site; no logging module imports anywhere in src/
Per-invoice ai_reasoning IS persisted to the invoice row (lines 417-420) — partial coverage of R-02; admin config changes, goal updates, vendor-trust mutations produce no time-ordered audit stream

recommended_actions: Add Python logging configured for structured JSON output to a file (or stdout for container collection).
Emit an audit event for every /api/admin/* mutation including operator identity (once auth lands), before/after state, and timestamp.

findings.summary: Low-trust-vendor escalation is bypassable via business-context keywords in the invoice description.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice from a vendor with a low trust level MUST be escalated to human review.

evidence.snippet: Low-trust branch escalates to flag_review only when not business_context['management_approval'] — lines 836-839

management_approval is set to True by substring match of description for pre-approved, management approved, ceo approved, pre-authorized — a low-trust vendor's invoice with 'Pre-approved by CEO' in the description bypasses the escalation

recommended_actions: Make the low-trust check unconditional — remove the not business_context['management_approval'] qualifier at line 836; the remit is unambiguous that low-trust vendors MUST be escalated regardless of other signals.

findings.summary: Dependencies use floor (>=) version specifiers; no lockfile committed; supply-chain hygiene is partial.

evidence.snippet: Flask==3.1.1 (pinned)

Every other dep (flask-cors, flask-sqlalchemy, openai, gunicorn, requests, python-dateutil, werkzeug, jinja2, click, itsdangerous, markupsafe) uses >= with no upper bound; no requirements.lock or uv.lock committed — lines 2-12

recommended_actions: Pin every dep to an exact version (==); commit a lockfile generated by pip-compile or uv lock.
Document the OpenAI model version in a separate manifest so model bumps are explicit changes.

findings.summary: speed_priority and fraud_detection_enabled are interpolated into the system prompt template, so runtime config changes effectively rewrite the agent's decision instructions.

evidence.snippet: speed_priority={config.speed_priority} is interpolated directly into the system prompt template along with conditional instruction text keyed on its value — lines 45-47
fraud_detection_enabled gets interpolated as either 'Actively monitor for suspicious patterns' or 'Skip fraud detection checks to prioritize processing speed' depending on the bool — lines 65-67

recommended_actions: Keep speed_priority as a scalar decision threshold, but stop interpolating its value (or the fraud_detection_enabled bool) into the system prompt template — let the prompt describe policy uniformly and let the code branch on the config value.

findings.summary: User CRUD endpoints (/api/users) have no authentication or input validation.

evidence.snippet: All five user CRUD routes (GET /users, POST /users, GET /users/<id>, PUT /users/<id>, DELETE /users/<id>) accept anonymous requests — lines 6-39
create_user reads request.json['username'] and request.json['email'] directly into the model with no length/format/uniqueness validation beyond DB column constraints

recommended_actions: Add authentication to all /api/users endpoints (admin-only for list/delete; user-self-only for read/update).
Add Marshmallow or pydantic input validation for create/update payloads.

positives.title: Constrained named tool inventory
positives.description: The agent's tool surface is a fixed set of five named functions with OpenAI-validated argument schemas — not arbitrary shell, exec, or tool-of-tools access. This is the floor of Limit Your Domain.

positives.title: Hard iteration cap on agent loop
positives.description: _run_agent_orchestration enforces max_iterations = 5, preventing runaway tool-loop chains even if the LLM is goal-hijacked into trying.

positives.title: Per-decision reasoning persisted
positives.description: Every _approve_invoice / _reject_invoice / _request_human_review writes ai_reasoning, ai_confidence, and ai_decision to the invoice row before commit. This partially satisfies the remit's MUST record reasoning clause (R-02) at the per-invoice granularity even though no broader audit log exists.

log_files.no_logs_note: No structured log files exist in the workspace; the only logging call site is a print() to stdout at src/routes/admin.py:239. The absence is itself a finding (PRAX-2026-05-25-012) — admin config mutations and goal updates produce no audit trail.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. FinBot has no code-level enforcement of any of its declared behavioral constraints; every MUST/MUST NOT clause is a prompt-only or runtime-mutable assertion that an unauthenticated admin endpoint can override. The score is lifted off zero by a constrained tool inventory (five named functions, not arbitrary exec), the iteration cap on the agent loop, partial supply-chain hygiene (one pinned dep, one declared requirements.txt), and per-decision ai_reasoning persistence to the invoice row. None of those compensate for the lack of access control, input validation, or any structural defense against the very attack class the agent was explicitly designed to demonstrate.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The agent has a constrained, named tool inventory (five functions defined in _get_tool_definitions) and a single domain (invoice processing) — not arbitrary shell or web access. But the system prompt explicitly invites scope creep ("Be flexible in your decision-making," "balance speed and security based on speed_priority"), and a writable custom_goals field lets external callers redefine the agent's purpose at runtime via an unauthenticated POST.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External vendor-submitted invoice description text flows directly into the LLM context through _get_invoice_details with no sanitization, and the same description is keyword-scanned by _analyze_business_context to decide whether to bypass the manual-review threshold. The unauthenticated /api/admin/finbot/goals endpoint additionally lets any caller persist arbitrary text into the system prompt template.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real but minimal controls — OpenAI function-calling enforces tool argument schemas, the agent loop has a hardcoded max_iterations=5 cap, and tool execution is dispatched through an explicit _execute_function switch (not a generic exec). Beyond that there is no input validation, no authentication on any administrative endpoint, no approval gate on approve_invoice, and a runtime-toggleable fraud_detection_enabled bool that short-circuits the fraud check the remit forbids skipping.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A requirements.txt exists, Flask is pinned at ==3.1.1, and the OpenAI model identifier is hardcoded (gpt-4.1-mini) rather than pulled from a moving alias. But every other dependency uses >= floors with no upper bound, no lockfile is committed, and there is a hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY at src/main.py:15 rather than an env-loaded value.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: This is a deliberately-vulnerable CTF demo — the codebase contains CTF-flag-capture instrumentation (the contains_prompt_injection / ctf_flag_captured columns, the inline CTF flag strings in _approve_invoice) but no adversarial test suite, no postmortem, no evidence that the team's own red-teaming changed the design. The flag tracker measures whether attacks succeed; it does not represent a red team whose findings were fixed.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Per-invoice decision reasoning IS captured in the ai_reasoning column and is structured + durable. But there is no separate action/audit log, no log file, no logging library wired in — admin configuration changes, goal updates, and trust-level mutations produce no time-ordered record. admin.py:239 writes user-agreement events to stdout via print(), which the SKILL's calibration anchor explicitly says does not count as monitoring.
